caveats: a: O*NET covers crop, nursery, and greenhouse farmworkers nationally, not NAICS 111998 or the fee-paid contract-growing subset.
a: No six-digit occupational employment mix was found, so the estimate is not directly wage weighted from observed 111998 employment.
a: The frozen labor-share input is null, so this task share cannot be translated into an industry labor-cost opportunity from this packet.
rho: Precision agriculture is not synonymous with AI, and much of the cited equipment is already adopted rather than part of the remaining opportunity.
rho: USDA adoption evidence emphasizes major row crops and farm-size categories, not the 111998 contract-growing lens.
rho: Weather, field connectivity, seasonal labor, biological variation, and contractor-mandated practices may slow implementation.
e: The production-contract incidence anchor is for all farms, and the seed-contract evidence is dated.
e: A repeat marketing contract does not qualify because it remains a sale of farmer-owned output rather than outsourced production service.
e: The frozen LMM firm-count input is missing, so even a bounded eligibility share cannot yield a defensible eligible-firm count.
s5: Land ownership transfer is not the same as operating-company control transfer.
s5: The USDA evidence covers all farms and farmland rather than NAICS 111998 or LMM production contractors.
s5: Aging alone does not establish sale intent, transferability, or an external control transaction.
q: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-enabled gross benefit in crop production contracts.
q: Contract terms can differ sharply by seed, crop, buyer concentration, supplied inputs, acreage, and performance incentives.
q: This estimate excludes ordinary marketing-contract and spot-market crop sellers under the lens.
d5: One-year acreage and production changes are supply outcomes affected by weather and inventories, not direct measures of constant-price demand.
d5: The 111998 crop mix is unusually heterogeneous and the eligible production-contract subset is not separately measured.
d5: Cannabis and hemp demand and regulation can be volatile and geographically fragmented.
o: Operator requirement is inferred from production tasks rather than observed as a five-year quantity.
o: Vertical integration by buyers would reduce demand for the lens firm even though physical crop production continues.
o: Autonomous machinery may reduce human labor more rapidly than it removes the need for an accountable operating company.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring production fees paid by seed companies, processors, or other crop owners under agreements specifying production practices, quantity or acreage, quality, and delivery; ordinary crop-sale revenue is excluded.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code is heterogeneous and overwhelmingly product farming, which is incoherent with the fixed outsourced-service lens. It is therefore narrowed to genuine fee-paid production-contract growing, the crop arrangement USDA distinguishes from product-focused marketing contracts; the narrowing is based on business-model coherence, not attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Spot-market growers; growers using only marketing contracts while retaining ownership and production control; direct-to-consumer farms; maple, hop, mint, hemp, spice, agave, and combination-crop farms that simply sell their own output; custom farming or management-only operations classified as agricultural support activities; shells, captive units, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market 111998 growers that repeatedly provide crop-production services under production contracts in which an external contractor owns the crop or key inputs, directs production, and pays the grower a fee; the most plausible included cases are contract seed growers and similar specialty-crop operators.

a.bridge: O*NET describes the broader crop/nursery/greenhouse farmworker occupation rather than the wage-weighted mix of eligible 111998 contract growers. Its task profile is transformed by treating records, planning, data analysis, crop monitoring, compliance, scheduling, and external communication as AI-exposed while excluding most harvesting, handling, repair, irrigation setup, and equipment operation. USDA's broader crop-farm evidence on digital agriculture corroborates exposure but includes major field crops; borrowing their more data-rich work likely biases this estimate upward for small, manual specialty-crop operations.
caveats: O*NET covers crop, nursery, and greenhouse farmworkers nationally, not NAICS 111998 or the fee-paid contract-growing subset.
No six-digit occupational employment mix was found, so the estimate is not directly wage weighted from observed 111998 employment.
The frozen labor-share input is null, so this task share cannot be translated into an industry labor-cost opportunity from this packet.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task diary from 20-30 LMM contract seed and specialty-crop growers, linked to job titles and hours, plus firm-level identification of already automated tasks, would materially move this estimate.
a.rationale: The work remains predominantly physical: O*NET assigns high importance to general physical activity, material handling, machine control, vehicle operation, inspection, and repair. AI can assist the smaller information layer—recordkeeping, planning, agronomic decision support, monitoring interpretation, documentation, and customer or regulatory communications—so the wage-weighted exposed share is bounded well below one-third.

caveats: One-year acreage and production changes are supply outcomes affected by weather and inventories, not direct measures of constant-price demand.
The 111998 crop mix is unusually heterogeneous and the eligible production-contract subset is not separately measured.
Cannabis and hemp demand and regulation can be volatile and geographically fragmented.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year volume commitments, renewal forecasts, and make-versus-contract plans from major seed companies and specialty-crop buyers, combined with commodity-level constant-quality acreage and output forecasts for the actual eligible-firm mix, would materially move this estimate.
d5.rationale: The eligible basket is niche contract growing across heterogeneous seeds and specialty crops, not broad food demand. A near-flat base is more defensible than extrapolating any single commodity: USDA reported 2025 hop acreage down 6% and maple output down 2%, while the NAICS basket also spans grass and hay seed, mint, spices, agave, tea, hemp, maple, and mixed crops. Contracted quantities can shift between independent growing, vertical integration, crops, and regions even when end demand is stable.

e.bridge: USDA measures contract use across all U.S. farms and commodities, not the share of LMM 111998 firms meeting this outsourced-service lens. The 2% of farms using production contracts in 2020 is used as an anchor, then widened upward because contracts are more prevalent among large farms and because 111998 includes seed crops, one of the few crop groups where USDA says production contracts are common. It is constrained downward because most crop contracts are marketing contracts—product sales that are ineligible. The all-farm denominator may understate LMM incidence, while inclusion of livestock production contracts overstates crop incidence; the net direction is uncertain.
caveats: The production-contract incidence anchor is for all farms, and the seed-contract evidence is dated.
A repeat marketing contract does not qualify because it remains a sale of farmer-owned output rather than outsourced production service.
The frozen LMM firm-count input is missing, so even a bounded eligibility share cannot yield a defensible eligible-firm count.
e.change_evidence: Commodity-level ARMS microdata or a USDA special tabulation identifying production-contract farms by 2022 NAICS 111998 and revenue or EBITDA band, supplemented by seed-company grower rosters, would materially move this estimate.
e.rationale: USDA explicitly distinguishes production contracts, where the farmer provides a service for a fee, from marketing contracts, where the farmer sells a commodity. Most 111998 establishments are therefore outside the lens; only a small production-contract subset, concentrated plausibly in seed growing, is eligible.

l.detail: SUSB scope excludes this activity, no IPS labor-compensation/output series at any level, and no other receipts source downloaded; labor_share left null (recorded gap).
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $299,458 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 11); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

o.bridge: The Census NAICS definition and O*NET task profile establish that the underlying output requires biological crop production, field work, equipment, inspection, and accountable decisions, but neither directly measures the year-five operator-required share for eligible contract growers. Those physical and compliance-bound activities are transformed into a high retained-operator share, allowing a small reduction for contractor vertical integration, self-service monitoring, or fully autonomous equipment. O*NET's broader crop/nursery population may overstate manual work for mechanized seed farms and therefore bias the estimate upward.
caveats: Operator requirement is inferred from production tasks rather than observed as a five-year quantity.
Vertical integration by buyers would reduce demand for the lens firm even though physical crop production continues.
Autonomous machinery may reduce human labor more rapidly than it removes the need for an accountable operating company.
o.change_evidence: Contractor make-versus-buy plans, autonomous-equipment deployment data, and interviews with insurers, regulators, and contracting buyers about whether unattended or software-only production could satisfy accountability requirements would materially move this estimate.
o.rationale: Software can improve decisions and automate documentation, but it cannot itself provide land, grow and harvest crops, maintain irrigation and machinery, manage biological risk, or accept contractual and regulatory accountability. Some monitoring and machine operation may become more autonomous, yet an operating entity remains necessary for nearly all surviving demand.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-enabled gross benefit in crop production contracts.
Contract terms can differ sharply by seed, crop, buyer concentration, supplied inputs, acreage, and performance incentives.
This estimate excludes ordinary marketing-contract and spot-market crop sellers under the lens.
q.change_evidence: Anonymized multi-year production contracts and renewal histories from growers and seed or specialty-crop contractors, including fee formulas, cost pass-through clauses, productivity audits, and repricing cadence, would materially move this estimate.
q.rationale: Eligible growers are generally paid a fee under a production contract, so an operator can retain near-term savings during a contract term. Over five years, contractor bargaining, renewal repricing, formula resets, performance comparisons across growers, and competitive bids should share a meaningful portion of visible cost reductions. The range leaves room for sticky multi-season fees at the high end and rapid buyer capture at the low end.

rho.bridge: USDA measures adoption of precision-agriculture hardware and data systems on broader U.S. crop farms, not five-year implementation of the presently unautomated AI-exposed tasks in 111998. The observed 52% midsize and 70% large-scale use of guidance systems, plus 68% large-scale use of yield/soil mapping, is translated downward because these are mature technologies, many eligible crops are niche or manual, and implementation also requires data capture, connectivity, integration, worker training, and customer approval. The field-crop population and mature-technology benchmark likely bias achievable AI implementation upward.
caveats: Precision agriculture is not synonymous with AI, and much of the cited equipment is already adopted rather than part of the remaining opportunity.
USDA adoption evidence emphasizes major row crops and farm-size categories, not the 111998 contract-growing lens.
Weather, field connectivity, seasonal labor, biological variation, and contractor-mandated practices may slow implementation.
rho.change_evidence: Five-year implementation roadmaps and realized labor-hour reductions from at least 15 contract seed, hop, mint, maple, hemp, and mixed-crop operators, together with vendor deployment logs by crop and farm size, would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: LMM operators have enough scale to adopt decision support, document automation, scheduling, and machine-vision monitoring, but specialty-crop heterogeneity and physical workflows limit rapid rollout. USDA also reports much lower adoption for smaller farms and strong variation by technology, supporting a wide range rather than a mature-field-crop adoption rate.

s5.bridge: USDA measures planned transfer of farmland acres and producer demographics across all U.S. agriculture, not qualifying control transfers of eligible 111998 contract-growing firms. The 5% of farmland acres planned to transfer to others in five years is used as a lower anchor, then adjusted modestly upward for control changes that can occur without a land sale and for an average producer age of 58.1. Acre-weighting overrepresents large tracts, while omission of business transfers through leases, entity interests, and succession can bias the anchor downward; wills and trusts may not constitute qualifying control transfers, so they are not counted directly.
caveats: Land ownership transfer is not the same as operating-company control transfer.
The USDA evidence covers all farms and farmland rather than NAICS 111998 or LMM production contractors.
Aging alone does not establish sale intent, transferability, or an external control transaction.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year cohort of ownership changes, asset sales, equity transfers, and successor appointments among contract seed and specialty-crop farms, verified through county deeds, UCC records, contractor novations, and interviews with agricultural lenders and brokers, would materially move this estimate.
s5.rationale: Near-term open-market farmland turnover is thin despite an aging producer base. Genuine business-control transfers should exceed outright land sales somewhat, but family continuity, leased acreage, and contractor approval make a high five-year transfer probability difficult to defend.

principal_driver: The defensible opportunity is concentrated in the decision, monitoring, and administrative layer of a small fee-paid contract-growing subset.
principal_weakness: Most establishments are product sellers rather than outsourced-service firms, and no defensible LMM firm-count input exists.
